tool.name: SOPA Segmentation
tool.command:
## in the for loop, if the segmentation fails on one patch, it doesn't show up and the data is saved with the successfully segmented patches. The next line ensures to catch the error. set -e && @CMD_UNZIP_ZARR@
mkdir -p output_dir &&
_run_seg() { PATCH_FLAG="\${1:+--patch-index \$1}"; sopa segmentation #if $method.method_name == 'cellpose' cellpose input/spatialdata.zarr --diameter $method.diameter #for $channel in str($method.channels).split(',') --channels '$channel' #end for --flow-threshold $method.flow_threshold --cellprob-threshold $method.cellprob_threshold --model-type '$method.model_type' #if $method.pretrained_model --pretrained-model '$method.pretrained_model' #end if --min-area $method.min_area @CMD_IMG_PREPROCESSING@
#else if $method.method_name == 'stardist' stardist input/spatialdata.zarr --model-type '$method.model_type' #if $method.local_model --local-model '$method.local_model' #end if --prob-thresh $method.prob_thresh --nms-thresh $method.nms_thresh #if str($method.channels) != '' --channels '$method.channels' #end if --min-area $method.min_area @CMD_IMG_PREPROCESSING@
#else if $method.method_name == 'comseg' comseg input/spatialdata.zarr --min-area $method.min_area
#else if $method.method_name == 'baysor' baysor input/spatialdata.zarr --min-area $method.min_area #if $method.scale --scale $method.scale #end if
#else if $method.method_name == 'proseg' proseg input/spatialdata.zarr $method.infer_presets #if str($method.prior_shapes_key) != '' --prior-shapes-key '$method.prior_shapes_key' #end if #if str($method.bins_key) != '' --bins-key '$method.bins_key' #end if
#else if $method.method_name == 'tissue' tissue input/spatialdata.zarr #if str($method.image_key) != '' --image-key '$method.image_key' #end if --level $method.level --mode '$method.mode' --kwargs '{ "expand_radius_ratio": $method.expand_radius_ratio, #if str($method.channel) != '' "channel": "$method.channel", #end if "clip_parameters": [$method.clip_parameters], "blur_kernel_size": $method.blur_kernel_size, "open_kernel_size": $method.open_kernel_size, "close_kernel_size": $method.close_kernel_size, "drop_threshold": $method.drop_threshold, "allow_holes": $method.allow_holes }'
#end if \$PATCH_FLAG; } &&
#if $method.method_name == 'proseg' or $method.method_name == 'tissue' echo "Running segmentation (no patch support)" && _run_seg && #else NUM_PATCHES=\$(python -c "import spatialdata as sd; s = sd.read_zarr('input/spatialdata.zarr'); print(s.shapes['image_patches'].shape[0] if 'image_patches' in s.shapes else (s.shapes['transcripts_patches'].shape[0] if 'transcripts_patches' in s.shapes else ''))") && if [ -n "\$NUM_PATCHES" ]; then echo "Found \$NUM_PATCHES patches, running per-patch segmentation" && for PATCH_IDX in \$(seq 0 \$((NUM_PATCHES - 1))); do echo "Processing patch \$PATCH_IDX/\$((NUM_PATCHES - 1))" && ## in the for loop, if the segmentation fails on one patch, it doesn't show up and the data is saved with the successfully segmented patches. The || ensures to catch the error. _run_seg \$PATCH_IDX || { echo "Segmentation failed on patch \$PATCH_IDX" >&2; exit 1; }; done && echo "Resolving patch conflicts" && sopa resolve #if $method.method_name == 'cellpose' cellpose #else if $method.method_name == 'stardist' stardist #else if $method.method_name == 'baysor' baysor --min-area $method.min_area #else if $method.method_name == 'comseg' comseg --min-area $method.min_area #end if input/spatialdata.zarr; else echo "No patches found, running segmentation on whole data" && _run_seg; fi; #end if &&
## Move to output directory mv "input/spatialdata.zarr" output_dir/ &&
@CMD_ZIP_ZARR@

param.name: method_name
param.label: Segmentation method
param.option: Cellpose - This can be done on all patches directly, or on one individual patch.
Stardist - This can be done on all patches directly, or on one individual patch.
Comseg - This can be done on all patches directly, or on one individual patch.
Baysor - This can be done on all patches directly, or on one individual patch.
Proseg - This needs to be done on a single patch as proseg is fast enough and doesn't require parallelization.
Tissue - This can be done only on objects with H&E staining

param.label: Expected cell diameter (pixels)
param.help: If 0, the diameter will be automatically estimated. However, providing a value can improve results.

param.label: Channel names (comma-separated)
param.help: If one channel, then provide just a nucleus channel. If two channels, this is the cytoplasm and then the nucleus channel

param.label: Flow threshold
param.help: Increase this threshold if cellpose is not returning as many ROIs as you would expect. Similarly, decrease this threshold if cellpose is returning too many ill-shaped ROIs.

param.label: Cell probability threshold
param.help: Decrease this threshold if cellpose is not returning as many ROIs as you would expect. Similarly, increase this threshold if cellpose is returning too ROIs particularly from dim areas.

param.label: The typical cell radius in microns
param.help: The configuration is inferred based on this parameter.

param.label: infer the proseg presets based on the columns of the transcripts dataframe?
Key of spdata object containing the prior cell boundaries [Only for Visium HD data]
param.help: If `'auto'`, use the latest performed segmentation (e.g., stardist or the 10X Genomics segmentation).

param.label: Key of spdata object containing the bin-by-gene table of gene counts [Only for Visium HD data]
Name of the image key to use for tissue segmentation
Level of the multiscale image to use for tissue segmentation
The ratio of the radius of the polygons that will be expanded.
Channel
param.help: The channel to use for the staining mode. If None, the maximum value of all channels is used.

param.label: Clip parameters
param.help: Parameters used to get the threshold used to clip the image before converting it to an 8-bit image (only used in 'staining' mode). The first parameter is the quantile, and the second is the divisor. By default, the threshold is the 90th quantile divided by 5.

param.label: The kernel size of the median blurring operation
The kernel size of the morphological opening operation.
The kernel size of the morphological closing operation.
Drop threshold
param.help: Polygons whose area is inferior to drop_threshold * total_slide_area will be removed.

param.label: Allow holes
param.help: If yes, the holes in the polygons will be kept. If no, the holes will be removed.

tool.help:
**What it does**
SOPA Segmentation performs cell segmentation on patched spatial omics data. It supports multiple segmentation methods: cellpose, stardist, comseg, baysor, proseg, and tissue segmentation. For methods supporting patching (cellpose, stardist, comseg, baysor), it will automatically detect the patches in the input SpatialData object and run the segmentation on each patch separately, before resolving the patch overlaps to return a single segmentation for the whole slide. For methods not supporting patching (proseg and tissue), it will run the segmentation on the whole slide directly.
**Technology-specific advice**
Sopa is designed to run on all spatial technologies at single-cell resolution, but some choices or parameters may be more or less suited to certain technologies.
-**Xenium or MERSCOPE or CosMX** For Xenium or MERSCOPE data, we recommend using a transcript-based segmentation tool, e.g., Baysor or Proseg. With the Xenium 5k panel, Proseg is recommended due to its fast results and high quality. If using Baysor, you can also use area=20 to filter cells with an area below 20 um². To filter low-quality cells, you can provide min_transcripts=20 to the aggregation.
-**Visium HD** The first option is to run Stardist on the H&E image. Note that, to do that, you'll need the full-resolution image. Else, if you have a prior 10X segmentation, you can directly go to the Proseg section. You can use proseg to use the bins information to improve the segmentation and aggregation. To do so, proseg needs a prior segmentation: you can either use stardist , or directly use the 10X Genomics default segmentation available on the recent Visium HD versions.
-**MACSima or PhenoCycler** For spatial proteomics, Cellpose is needed. To avoid having artifacts outside of the tissue, we recommend running sopa.segmentation.tissue before running Cellpose. This way, Cellpose will run only inside the tissue. For MACSima, we recommend to use diameter=35 pixels and min_area=400 pixels² to sopa.segmentation.cellpose. For PhenoCycler data, it will depend on the image resolution, so you'll need to choose a diameter (in pixels) that is biologically relevant. During aggregation, you can for instance use expand_radius_ratio=0.1 to expand the cells, min_intensity_ratio=0.1 to filter cells with a too low intensity.
-**H&E or WSI** If you have only a H&E/WSI slide without spatial omics, you can also use Sopa, although many operations are dedicated to spatial omics. For instance, sopa.segmentation.tissue for tissue segmentation, sopa.segmentation.stardist for cell segmentation. Though, if you have H&E with spatial omics, e.g. Xenium + H&E, in that case, we recommend segmenting the data on the spatial transcriptomics and aligning the H&E to performed joined operations.
**Performance Notes**
- Segmentation can be time-consuming for large datasets - For very large images, consider using smaller patches or reducing patch overlap
**Multi-step segmentation** Multi-step segmentation consists of running multiple times Cellpose over the whole slides with different parameters. For instance, we can first run a nucleus segmentation using DAPI, then another round using DAPI and a membrane staining, and finally, DAPI and cell boundary staining. This can make the segmentation more robust. Note that the results of the multiple steps are combined into one final segmentation (You need to run Sopa resolve tool after that).
**More Information**
See the `SOPA segmentation documentation <https://prism-oncology.github.io/sopa/tutorials/cli_usage/#run-segmentation>`_.
